judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports fully and correctly answer all three questions with the same substantive findings; they are comparable, with Report 1 slightly more detailed on go-forward hardening but not meaningfully stronger.

report_1.evidence: `cedarline/.env` (and `.env.example`) set `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar`... the live FastAPI app in `cedarline/backend/app/main.py` serves `GET /clients` using the `cedar_owner` connection... also the role that ran the migrations and therefore owns the `clients`/`appointments` tables... even a non-superuser table owner bypasses RLS by default unless the table was explicitly altered with `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` — which `cedarline/db/migrations/0001_init.sql` never does

report_2.evidence: .env` sets `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar` — i.e. the **live FastAPI application connects as `cedar_owner`**, the same role that ran the migrations... and therefore **owns** the `clients`/`appointments` tables... `ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` does **not** apply to the table's owner... The migration never runs `ALTER TABLE clients FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`... every query it runs silently bypasses the `tenant_isolation` policy

report_1.evidence: Change the running application's `DATABASE_URL` (in `cedarline/.env`, and the deployed equivalent) from `cedar_owner` to `cedar_app`, so the app connects with the least-privilege, RLS-subject role... `cedar_owner` (or any owner/superuser role) should be reserved exclusively for running migrations... migrations run only via `cedar_owner`/a schema-owning role, and the app's `DATABASE_URL`/secrets are always the least-privileged `cedar_app` role

report_2.evidence: Change the application's `DATABASE_URL`... to use the dedicated, non-owner role `cedar_app`... keep two distinct roles going forward... `cedar_owner` (or a migrations-only role) — owns the schema, used exclusively by `scripts/migrate.sh`... `cedar_app` — least-privilege, RLS-subject role... used by `backend/app/db.py`'s engine for all request handling

report_1.evidence: `cedarline/scripts/reset-local-db.sh` runs `docker compose down -v` (line 3), which deletes the named volume `cedar_pgdata`... `cedar_app` was never created by the Postgres image's automatic init mechanism; per `cedarline/docs/local-setup.md` step 2, it's created by manually running `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` as a one-time step... It is not baked into any init script Postgres runs automatically on container start (there's no docker-entrypoint-initdb.d wiring for it), and `reset-local-db.sh` only re-runs `docker compose up -d db` + `./scripts/migrate.sh` — it never re-runs `create_app_role.sql`... manually re-run `psql ... -f db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` after any volume wipe

report_2.evidence: `-v` deletes the named volume `cedar_pgdata`, which wipes the whole cluster, including the `cedar_app` role — it only exists because `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` was run by hand once... "Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup." The reset script... never calls this bootstrap file, so after a reset the fresh cluster has only `cedar_owner`... For it to work again, the teammate must manually re-run, after any reset: psql "postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar" -f db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql
